text: Here’s a list of books on the death penalty that cover both supporting and critical perspectives:  Dead Man Walking by Sister Helen Prejean https://amzn.to/3WLGDgM – A personal and ethical exploration of capital punishment in the U.S., highlighting its human impact.  The Death Penalty: A Worldwide Perspective by Roger Hood and Carolyn Hoyle  https://amzn.to/4nRrx5p – Provides a comparative analysis of death penalty laws, practices, and trends across the globe.  Gideon’s Trumpet by Anthony Lewis https://amzn.to/3KTF5yT – Focuses on landmark legal cases that shaped the death penalty and defendants’ rights in the U.S.  These books provide a mix of ethical, legal, historical, and global perspectives on the death penalty, making them great resources for a comprehensive understanding.

I believe the death penalty is a problematic form of justice. While some argue it deters serious crimes, there is no conclusive evidence that it effectively reduces crime rates. It is irreversible, and wrongful convictions can lead to innocent lives being lost. Moreover, it often reflects systemic inequalities, disproportionately affecting marginalized communities. Ethical concerns about state-sanctioned killing also challenge its morality. Instead of capital punishment, life imprisonment with rehabilitation opportunities could serve justice while respecting human dignity and minimizing irreversible mistakes.

There was a very strange feature in this case, strange because of its extremely rare occurrence. This man had once been brought to the scaffold in company with several others, and had had the sentence of death by shooting passed upon him for some political crime. Twenty minutes later he had been reprieved and some other punishment substituted; but the interval between the two sentences, twenty minutes, or at least a quarter of an hour, had been passed in the certainty that within a few minutes he must die. I was very anxious to hear him speak of his impressions during that dreadful time, and I several times inquired of him as to what he thought and felt. He remembered everything with the most accurate and extraordinary distinctness, and declared that he would never forget a single iota of the experience. ‘About twenty paces from the scaffold, where he had stood to hear the sentence, were three posts, fixed in the ground, to which to fasten the criminals (of whom there were several). The first three criminals were taken to the posts, dressed in long white tunics, with white caps drawn over their faces, so that they could not see the rifles pointed at them. Then a group of soldiers took their stand opposite to each post. My friend was the eighth on the list, and therefore he would have been among the third lot to go up. A priest went about among them with a cross: and there was about five minutes of time left for him to live. ‘He said that those five minutes seemed to him to be a most interminable period, an enormous wealth of time; he seemed to be living, in these minutes, so many lives that there was no need as yet to think of that last moment, so that he made several arrangements, dividing up the time into portions—one for saying farewell to his companions, two minutes for that; then a couple more for thinking over his own life and career and all about himself; and another minute for a last look around. He remembered having divided his time like this quite well. While saying good- bye to his friends he recollected asking one of them some very usual everyday question, and being much interested in the answer. Then having bade farewell, he embarked upon those two minutes which he had allotted to looking into himself; he knew beforehand what he was going to think about. He wished to put it to himself as quickly and clearly as possible, that here was he, a living, thinking man, and that in three minutes he would be nobody; or if somebody or something, then what and where? He thought he would decide this question once  for all in these last three minutes. A little way off there stood a church, and its gilded spire glittered in the sun. He remembered staring stubbornly at this spire, and at the rays of light sparkling from it. He could not tear his eyes from these rays of light; he got the idea that these rays were his new nature, and that in three minutes he would become one of them, amalgamated somehow with them. ‘The repugnance to what must ensue almost immediately, and the uncertainty, were dreadful, he said; but worst of all was the idea, ‘What should I do if I were not to die now? What if I were to return to life again? What an eternity of days, and all mine! How I should grudge and count up every minute of it, so as to waste not a single instant!’ He said that this thought weighed so upon him and became such a terrible burden upon his brain that he could not bear it, and wished they would shoot him quickly and have done with it.’¹

When I was in college I had the choice of a few topics for my research paper. The topics available were abortion, death penalty and welfare. I chose what I thought was the least controversial topic, the death penalty,  I was very pro death penalty until I completed my research. When reviewing several death penalty cases I was a little surprised at my findings. In many cases, unless the defendant admitted to the crime, there was video evidence that clearly showed the defendant committing the crime there were problems with witness accounts, not having the body of the victim, police manufacturing evidence, prosecutors not sharing evidence with the defense, jurors who show no interest in the trial and judges that are in the courtroom but a million miles away.   What I found was most appalling was the manufacture of evidence by the police to secure a conviction and prosecutorial misconduct by the DAs office especially in high profile cases and ill-equipped defense attorneys who barely graduated law school and end up as public defenders. These public defenders are appointed by the judge to represent the defendant if he  is unable to afford an attorney.    Then there's the work of the Innocence Project. These are law students who take on cases when they believe there is an issue with the trial.   As I stated I was very pro death penalty but after my paper I decided I could no longer support the death penalty. In Texas, for decades, there was no life without parole that's why there were so many sentenced to death. Several years ago the legislature passed a life without parole thereby reducing the number of those executed.   George Bush, the younger, was president he stated firmly that he is 100% sure that everyone who was sentenced to death and executed since the days of either the republic or since becoming a state had a fair, lawful and just trial.   Now than, that's a very bold statement to make. Considering he's never looked at every single death penalty case and execution in Texas going back to its admission as a state.   That statement made me think twice about how the death penalty is applied. As I stated I no longer support the death penalty because as much as we want to believe that everyone in the entire USA will get a fair trial from their time in traffic court to the most awful crime of murder not every one gets a fair trail. I'm extremely against the death penalty 1.There is no credible proof that the death penalty works as a deterrent. In the US, in states where the death penalty has been abolished, there has been no significant change in the rates for serious criminal offenses, such as murder. 2. It is a cruel and unusual punishment, where basic standards of human dignity are compromised or undermined. It continues the cycle of violence. Retribution is just another word for revenge—it is essentially just a form of the flawed thinking that two wrongs can make a right. The pro argument is that killing people is wrong; therefore, you should kill people for killing, which makes no sense. . . 3. It affects the poorer segments of society and racial minorities disproportionately, in part because they cannot afford the costs of good legal support. In the USA, although only 13% of the population is African-American, 50% of death row prisoners are African-American. 4. The justice system is bound to make mistakes. In the case of people who are wrongly imprisoned, they can be released from prison and given compensation, but a wrongful execution can never be righted. 5. The death penalty is not cost-effective. When all the practical and legal costs are taken into account, it is clear that the execution is more expensive than imprisoning for life. 6. A life spent in prison is a worse punishment than an execution. A life sentence prisoner has many years to endure their punishment, as well as experience remorse and reflect on his or her crimes. of law enforcement officials. The abolition of the death penalty occurs most often in states where the murder of police officers is a very low percentage of all homicides. In 2014, there were 14,000 murders that took place in the United States, but there were only 35 executions that took place. 7. It is used to control political messages. The United States uses the death penalty exclusively for the punishment of crimes as defined by legal code and precedent. It is a principle which is not consistent for other countries in the world. 78% of global executions because of capital punishment come from just four countries when excluding China: Iraq, Vietnam, Saudi Arabia, and Iran. 8. Family members of a victim are adversely impacted by the death penalty. The Marquette Law Review found that when family members go through the capital punishment process after someone they love becomes a victim, they have higher levels of mental, physical, and behavioral health problems when compared to when the perpetrator receives a sentence of life in prison. Although this issue does not happen in every circumstance, some family members can feel responsible for the fact that the government is putting this criminal to death.     Proponents would argue that capital punishment provides relief because it guarantees that person can no longer harm another, but there are many families who do not feel a sense of satisfaction with this action. If they are the ones who experience loss, then there should be a way to provide input for them. There are very few prison escapes that occur, and fewer that involve violent criminals. 9. The number of escapes from prison in the United States declined by more than 50% between 1998-2013, falling to a rate of 10.5 escapes per 10,000 prisoners in 2013. At the same time, the number of life sentences handed out by the court system has gone up by 500%. Most of the incidents that contribute to a prisoner escape come from low-security situations, like when 16 prisoners walked away from a work site and another 3 disappeared from a community work center.  Out of all of the reported escapes in 2013, only one inmate from a secured facility was able to get away.     Some countries, including Sudan and Iran, use the death penalty as a political tool. It becomes a way to punish political opponents who might want to take their country in a different direction. There were a total of 2,500 death sentences recorded in 54 countries in 2018, with about 20,000 people currently under sentence around the world at the end of the year.  10. Children are sometimes put to death through the use of capital punishment. There are at least 97 kids who were put to death by capital punishment laws in Iran since 1990. Another 145 child executions have happened in China, the Republic of Congo, Pakistan, Sudan, South Sudan, Yemen, Nigeria – and the United States.  Scott Hain, Toronto Patterson, T.J. Jones, Napoleon Beazley, Gerald Mitchell, Shaka Sankofa, Glen McGinnis, and Steven Roach were all put to death in the United States for a crime that they committed at age 17. Sean Sellers was executed when he was 29 for a crime that he committed when he was 16. 11. The evidence obtained to justify the death penalty is sometimes tainted. There are specific guidelines in the United States today that limit how and when law enforcement can obtain evidence during questioning. This structure of protection is not always available around the world. There are numerous people executed after being convicted during an unfair trial, often on the basis of evidence or confessions obtained through the use of torture. Some defendants were not given adequate legal representation.  Some countries even use the death penalty as a mandatory punishment for specific offenses, which means the judge cannot consider the circumstances of the crime during the sentencing phase of a trial. 12. It’s an interesting and compelling debate. I sometimes think societies get the murderers they deserve and punish them accordingly. As I’m from the UK, it always appears to me that the era of hanging the guilty is one which remains in the past and should stay there. Likewise France was guillotining up to the early 1970’s and similarly Spain were still garrotting people at the same time. Only Belarus remain as the last bastion of capital punishment within Europe. Even Russia have abolished the death penalty. The only major countries left in the world who carry out executions regularly are China, Iran, Iraq, Indonesia (and some other South East Asian states) and the US. Clearly the punishments don’t work as a deterrent otherwise the murder rate would be zero. The US by comparison with Europe, taken as a whole is still a violent country which is still quite comfortable letting its citizens roam around with guns. If you’re caught carrying a gun in Europe you are quite likely to go to prison or worse still be perceived as a terrorist and shot. Our societies are clearly different and until the US gets a grip on violence then the death penalty may be your only hope of keeping a lid on things. Obviously that comes with all sorts of problems, the quality of representation in court, the lengthy appeals process and the pathetic witnesses to the execution at the termination of someone’s life. Certainly if Russia saw fit to abolish the death penalty and introduce a harsh reign of imprisonment instead then it should be possible for the US to do likwise but right this moment I actually think it enjoys having the death penalty as it squares with a macho image some states are hoping to project. Ultimately it isn’t a deterrent, it isn’t humane and it debases the society who practices it. If you fail to understand that then sadly you are part of the problem.

A: Tuff shit. B: Abolotionist statement:  "It's cheaper to house a convict for life than to execute that convict."  That's not the biggest pile of bull shit I've ever heard, but it's close. Ya know, I don't have any figures but we'll assume a couple of numbers for demonstration purposes.  How many people are now sentenced to life without parole?  How fast are people who are convicted of a capital crime skipping out by joining those ranks as we pass out life without parole sentences like giving away Halloween candy?  How far down the road is anyone actually visualizing?  Okay.  What is the median age of all those who have been sentenced to this punishment?  Let's assume the range is between 22 and 45.  When I was between these years I was in shape*.  Active but not pumping iron in a prison yard.  I ate nutritious food whenever I could.  Not a health nut but had a rather active life working for the railroad.  In a few months I'll be 67.  I have eleven stints in those bad veins around my heart.  This is the total of three separate procedures.  With Medicare, one of the prescriptions I need is $550 a month as my co-pay.  They don’t say that in the Humira commercials.  The “starter pack” was $13,000 and my co-pay was a few dollars short of $2,000.  This is an example, but also my point.  Health care costs are usurious.  Okay.  Here’s where the bullshit starts.  How many thousands are currently serving this no parole term?  2,000?  15,000 or more?  Who knows.  How many of those convicts are people who average between 22 and 45 years of age?  I’m sure the number is quite high. Unless they get shanked they can get to 67 years old too.  Health care costs are not considered by the abolitionists who try to circumvent State or Federal law citing the untrue economic ramifications.  I voted to re-instate the death penalty in California.  As part of incarceration for a day or a lifetime adequate health care must be provided with pharmacological prescriptions at a bare minimum.  If not that would fit the definition of cruel and unusual punishment.  You cannot disallow medical treatment.  That is the reality.  Wasn’t Manson approaching his eighty’s when he checked out?  25 years from now there will be thousands and thousands of geriatrics and their medicinal needs will be fulfilled from a public treasury.  And who funds a public treasury?  Yup.  Taxpayers.  That’s you and me.  Co-pay is moot.  There will be none since the prison system anywhere is going to pay in full.  Which includes you and me.  And about this time that million dollars saved 40 years ago, at that time wherein the punishment didn’t fit the crime, is going to loom large and doubling or tripling that cost at a bare minimum, times 20,000 or more convicts with thousands more joining those ranks yearly?  Are there moral implications?  Not my job to decide.  I’m no theologian but in some circumstances I’m pretty sure killing is allowed in the Bible, the Talmud and for certain in the Koran.  Final judgment by God?  Okay.  Then all execution does is to allow for an impending arrival to that state of grace be achieved sooner rather than later.  So, aren’t we actually doing the condemned a favor?  There is one culture I read about, somewhere in the remote Pacific Ocean or the African continent, a tribal society.  In their culture, someone convicted of murder is turned over to the victim’s next of kin, and they mete out the justice as they see fit.  There is a very low murder rate.   Would I like to be the one that throws the switch?  I’d rather be doing something else on any given day but if it furthers my sense of earthly justice then I would do so with a clear conscience and in compliance with the law.  We call this thing of ours a “Judicial” system, derived from the root word of the word justice, but it isn’t.  There is rarely justice.  What we have is a “Legal” system.  The two are not the same.  Wrongly convicted, have innocent people been put to death in the past?  Absolutely.  I have no doubt.  And others who have been on death row have been exonerated before that sentence was carried out.  Why?  Modern DNA evidence cleared them.  It’s the 21st Century.  And modern DNA evidence is convicting them these days.  Slam dunk.  No appeal.  You’re caught.  Period.  And now you gotta go.  As for method of carrying out a just sentence it’s too complicated as it now stands.  Never mind multiple drugs for lethal injection.  Conserve electrical energy.  Save your bullets, your rope and your cyanide.  A massive overdose of heroine is efficient, cheap and very humane, as the condemned get to check out feeling pretty good about things.   *The shape was that of an avocado

1. There's a lot of space between the death penalty and a punishment that would be appropriate for speeding. Life in prison is a valid option. 2. I would argue that the point of punishment shouldn't be to demonstrate one's opinion on the morality of an action, at least not directly or primarily. But rather, it should be to reduce the likelihood of that action being done. 4. While I can understand how the family of a murder victim would want the perpetrator dead, I don't think making someone feel better is worth more than a person's life. (And besides, they can at least take solace in the knowledge that the perpetrator will most likely spend the rest of their life in prison where they can't hurt anyone else and where they probably don't want to be.) 5. There's no such thing as "absolute proof." When DNA testing was first developed and put into practice, many convicts whose guilt was previously considered absolutely, 100% certain were revealed to be innocent, including some who had already been put to death. As long as capital punishment is a thing we do _at all,_ it _will_ be done to innocent people, no matter _how_ sure we are that this time, we really, truly, absolutely _know_ they're guilty. (Of course, the same is true of any punishment, but we can always release someone who was wrongly imprisoned. We can't resurrect someone who was wrongly executed.)  6. Wasn't "thou shalt not kill" one of the Ten Commandments? 7. I don't think we should make exceptions if the crime in question is just _that_ bad, because which crimes qualify as "just _that_ bad" is completely subjective.

The death penalty is a tricky subject. Personally, I have no issue with the death penalty in some situations. I feel that capital punishment should not be used as loosely as it is in the states that still practice it. I feel that it should only be reserved for the most savage and depraved of killers. If a guy is committing a robbery and he shoots someone in a moment of either desperation or panic, then I don’t think he should get the death penalty. Life imprisonment? Absolutely. However, if a guy breaks into an apartment and tortures a couple, cuts off fingers, stabs them not to kill but to inflict pain, beats them with either a blunt object or his own fists, and then shoots or stabs them to death after he gets bored, then I absolutely believe that he should be sentenced to death. Do I believe that some people on death row shouldn’t be there? Yes. I’m not the authority on any of this, but in my opinion, the system should reform the way capital punishment works. There was a very strange feature in this case, strange because of its extremely rare occurrence. This man had once been brought to the scaffold in company with several others, and had had the sentence of death by shooting passed upon him for some political crime. Twenty minutes later he had been reprieved and some other punishment substituted; but the interval between the two sentences, twenty minutes, or at least a quarter of an hour, had been passed in the certainty that within a few minutes he must die. I was very anxious to hear him speak of his impressions during that dreadful time, and I several times inquired of him as to what he thought and felt. He remembered everything with the most accurate and extraordinary distinctness, and declared that he would never forget a single iota of the experience. ‘About twenty paces from the scaffold, where he had stood to hear the sentence, were three posts, fixed in the ground, to which to fasten the criminals (of whom there were several). The first three criminals were taken to the posts, dressed in long white tunics, with white caps drawn over their faces, so that they could not see the rifles pointed at them. Then a group of soldiers took their stand opposite to each post. My friend was the eighth on the list, and therefore he would have been among the third lot to go up. A priest went about among them with a cross: and there was about five minutes of time left for him to live. ‘He said that those five minutes seemed to him to be a most interminable period, an enormous wealth of time; he seemed to be living, in these minutes, so many lives that there was no need as yet to think of that last moment, so that he made several arrangements, dividing up the time into portions—one for saying farewell to his companions, two minutes for that; then a couple more for thinking over his own life and career and all about himself; and another minute for a last look around. He remembered having divided his time like this quite well. While saying good- bye to his friends he recollected asking one of them some very usual everyday question, and being much interested in the answer. Then having bade farewell, he embarked upon those two minutes which he had allotted to looking into himself; he knew beforehand what he was going to think about. He wished to put it to himself as quickly and clearly as possible, that here was he, a living, thinking man, and that in three minutes he would be nobody; or if somebody or something, then what and where? He thought he would decide this question once  for all in these last three minutes. A little way off there stood a church, and its gilded spire glittered in the sun. He remembered staring stubbornly at this spire, and at the rays of light sparkling from it. He could not tear his eyes from these rays of light; he got the idea that these rays were his new nature, and that in three minutes he would become one of them, amalgamated somehow with them. ‘The repugnance to what must ensue almost immediately, and the uncertainty, were dreadful, he said; but worst of all was the idea, ‘What should I do if I were not to die now? What if I were to return to life again? What an eternity of days, and all mine! How I should grudge and count up every minute of it, so as to waste not a single instant!’ He said that this thought weighed so upon him and became such a terrible burden upon his brain that he could not bear it, and wished they would shoot him quickly and have done with it.’

My opposition to the death penalty is one part pragmatism and one part trying to do God’s will as I understand it.  Pragmatically, I have found it much easier to persuade people in the Pro-Life direction when I don’t have to include nuances like innocent vs. criminal life (whereas killing in immediate self-defense is pretty easy to argue for; you’re choosing innocent lives over the life of the attacker, but still choosing life)… “all life needs to be protected” is much harder for those on the fence to refute when you’re trying to sway them. I prioritize saving innocent lives in danger of death over putting criminals who can be punished in other ways to death.  In terms of faith; as I understand it, God wishes for all of us to be with Him in Heaven. But for those killers to be with God they must first repent and by putting the criminal to death you deny them any future chance to be able to do so. Maybe they never do, but do we really want to stand before God and tell him, “I supported executing this person so they could never be reconciled with You?”  And you never know what the future might hold… Saint Maria Goretti at the age of 12 was stabbed 14 times while struggling against her would be rapist and, despite the heinous attack, on her deathbed proclaimed her wish for her attacker to repent so he could reach Heaven. Her attacker was unrepentant for three years, but after a dream/vision did repent and served the remaining 27 years of his sentence as a model prisoner (he was not sentenced to life because he was considered a minor at the time), whereupon he went immediately to beg Maria’s mother for forgiveness (which was granted), joined a monastery and spent the rest of his life in service to others. If he had been executed, such redemption would not have been possible and Saint Maria’s dying wish would have gone unfulfilled.  So there’s my two reasons for opposing the death penalty that I hope are understandable; that it is easier to persuade those on the Pro-Life fence by avoiding the need for nuanced arguments and because when you kill another, even if they might deserve it, you deny them the chance for repentance with God.

Is capital punishment a deterrent?  No it isn't.  Capital punishment in the United Kingdom was, of course, abolished in the 1960s. But prior to the abolition of capital punishment, the UK's most prolific executioner was Albert Pierrepoint (1905-1992). He conducted more than 400 executions during his career, including those of over 200 Nazi war criminals, and also William Joyce (the last person in the UK to be executed for treason), as well as serial killers such as John Haigh and John Christie, but later in life he became an opponent of capital punishment.  One execution that made him change his mind was that of James Corbitt in 1950. Corbitt knew the hangman long before he committed the offence – he had been a regular in the pub that Pierrepoint owned, and was well aware that Pierrepoint was also an executioner. On the night that Corbitt murdered his girlfriend (the crime for which he was sentenced to death), the killer and executioner had sung a duet in the pub. Corbitt's son actually remarked in 2008 that his father "probably deserved the hangman's noose" on the basis that he'd been planning the murder for more than a year.  However, the case shows that capital punishment is not a deterrent. This was the conclusion reached by Pierrepoint, as Corbitt was well aware that his local pub landlord was also an executioner. This should have deterred him from committing murder, let alone one that he had planned for such a long time. But it didn't.

Ufff... I love Prager U, but this one wasn't so good. Sure, some murderers deserve the death penalty by their actions. But there actually have been cases of innocent people suffering the death penalty undeservedly. Prager tried side-stepping that problem by restricting the discussion to the truly guilty. But even assuming that we could know for certain that someone was guilty, and not just guilty, but guilty to the point of deserving the death penalty, then we should still uphold the sanctity of human life by not taking human life. It's meant to set an example: We don't take human life if it can be avoided. Now, as Prager mentioned, one CAN take a human life in the case of legitimate self defense. And perhaps in the distant past the death penalty was justifiable insofar as it was the only way to keep society safe from murderers. But today we have high security prisons that eliminate that concern. And I agree with Prager that it would devalue human life to give a murderer the same sentence as a person who breaks the speed limit. But murderers don't get speeding tickets. They get life in prison (or at least they should). And if they're not getting life in prison, then the solution is not to kill them, but to make them die in prison. I'll like to see a counter-video by Alan Dershowitz, who has contributed to Prager U on the topic of Israel but has written on the death penalty elsewhere.

Before I say anything, I just wanna say that I'm not really invested in a side or anything. I mainly just wanted to talk about the whole "do we have the right to kill" thing. This is just one of many views here, but I think that every person born into a civilized nation is granted the status of being that's just a little higher than your average animal (and we call that a human). As long as you follow the rules of society, you can remain in it. As with all rules, some rules are to be upheld at all costs. Naturally, this includes the intentional, unjust murder of an innocent person. If someone breaks one of those rules, they can say goodbye to ever being truly "human" again. If they killed a random innocent person, society itself will reject them. If the person hasn't lost their humanity yet, that guilt will probably crush them until they finally lose it. At that point, it becomes a matter of exchange. While you can never return the deceased, you can get rid of the dangerous human-looking animals before they cause any more harm. No one would complain if a lion was put down for going on a human killing spree. Of course, there will be times when an innocent person might be wrongly convicted, but that's kind of unrelated here. That's something that the court system needs to improve on. If they couldn't find a way to prove the man innocent, then he's guilty. If you have a problem with it, then take it to the judicial system, not the death penalty.  Of course, this is looking at the whole argument from a logical standpoint. When it comes to how systems should work, you should never bring emotion into it. Doing so will just cloud judgment and lead to controversies like this when they shouldn't really be here in the first place. If you look at it from a single person's view, it's much clearer. If a person takes a life, they should be prepared to lose theirs too (and that's regardless of the kind of killing that occurred. Soldiers kill in war, but they (ideally) do so with the understanding that they can die out there.  In the end, though, I don't think it matters that much. As long as peace is maintained in society, things can remain as they are. It's only when things get messy that change should be introduced (You know, the whole "If it ain't broke, don't fix it" notion). The people fighting against the death penalty don't really care for the person dying there, but rather they feel for the fact that the country is intentionally killing another person. They don't personally know that person (unless they do, of course). As long as they don't personally know the person, then fighting against the death penalty isn't really impactful. You can't fight an emotional battle with logic, after all.   Just as one last thing though, I really don't get why people use the whole "innocent person" argument. Like yeah, it's bad to kill an innocent person, but the reason they're there is that they weren't proven innocent in their trial(s) (once again don't know how that really works). Logically speaking, the judicial component of that whole ordeal should completely filter out any innocent people. If innocent people are put down, then the blame lies pretty much entirely with their defense for not being able to unveil the truth (or the opposition for fighting for the sake of winning and not the sake of unveiling the truth).   Those are just my views, though, and since I probably lack 85% of the information to make a fully cohesive and accurate argument, this will have to do. I'm really only here for a school project, so I really didn't know anything about the subject when I first looked into it. After all of that, I can say with utmost confidence that this argument makes even less sense than when I first started. It's like looking at the whole "the Earth is Round vs the Earth is Flat" argument. That's just how it looks like to an outsider, though, so people arguing for the death penalty's removal, here's a message: make better arguments! You're not gonna be able to topple first-hand logic with arguments like that! (That's just coming from an outsider, though. It is a complex subject that Dennis Prager had oversimplified: (1) Dennis Prager chose a heinous crime to shock his audience and elicit a strong emotional reaction that would cloud reason. (2) Once applied, the death penalty is irreversible and cannot be modified. You must therefore know precisely what crimes merit the death penalty, and why. In practice, lawyers who support the death penalty can and do argue about this endlessly, and the general population even more so. Where do YOU draw the line in different scenarios and under different circumstances, and why should YOU be the one to draw it? (3) Once there is general agreement about the definitions of crimes that merit the death penalty, you will find that many crimes are not easily classified according to those definitions, and some seem to "straddle the fence". (4) Only fools think that diagnoses of mental illnesses are clearcut, on the basis of the precise definitions of psychiatrists who are basing the syndromes they define on the basis of decades of precise, scientific research. No! The human mind isn't like that, and it is impossible to truly know what it is to be another person. For example, there may be a legal definition of the mental age or IQ at which an individual may be held legally responsible for their actions as an adult, but that mental age/IQ may differ depending on the test/s used to define it, and indeed in repeated tests. The definition of the mental age of full legal responsibility is itself arbitrary and differs from country to country, and it can also be impossible to tell how an individual's mental age/IQ changes their susceptibility to be duped or misled by other people. What is more, it is impossible to judge the degree to which certain mental conditions, such as post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), may cloud a person's judgment and affect their actions. (5) In many cases it is impossible to say that all juries would arrive at the same verdict, and that all judges would apply the same sentence. People differ in their home cultures, upbringing, and life experience, and some things that may at first sight be viewed as trivial, can subtly influence the way people judge others. Think, for example, of how we choose life partners. (6) Dennis Prager is simply wrong to say that there is an infinitesimally small chance that given all the checks and balances in the legal system, someone sentenced to death is actually innocent. Some years ago the Harvard Legal Review published an excellent article by an outstanding lawyer, who had decided to strive to the best of his ability and using all available statistics, to determine how many people in the USA are wrongfully executed in the age of DNA evidence. (I downloaded his paper and have it on an old computer.) One of many problems is that in some cases we never discover that the people executed were in fact innocent. However, he found mathematical ways around this and other problems, and if my memory serves me correctly, he concluded that roughly 3% of all people executed in the USA, are executed wrongfully, i.e. they suffer judicial murder. The lawyer contrasted this with the probable number of lives potentially saved and crimes prevented by executing prisoners instead of applying life sentences. He concluded that the conclusion each of us arrives at depends on how much weight we personally assign to the execution of murderers, versus the societal crime of committing judicial murders. (7) The lawyer in the previous point didn't stop there, though. He proved that some people who have committed terrible crimes, do reform themselves, and indeed earn partial or complete redemption through acts later in life which give back to society. Death is an irreversible penalty that does not allow this.  (8) Having said this, there is a strong argument that in the USA the death penalty is not only a death penalty, but invariably also includes a prison sentence of indeterminate duration followed by death. The fact that society sentences other people to prison only, means that we regard imprisonment to be a punishment in itself. In that case, someone sentenced to death and executed after 3 years is not receiving the same penalty as someone sentenced to death and executed after 10 years. Is this just? (9) Is the death penalty a fitting sentence? If you have ever been anesthetized for an operation, you may well doubt that execution is a suitable punishment for many crimes. Is it not much too easy? Aren't many ailing people who are suffering intolerably, desperate for voluntary euthanasia to be legal? Why should we grant sadistic killers such easy deaths while denying such deaths to innocent people who desire them? Is it not much more appropriate to sentence such criminals to life in prison in horribly uncomfortable conditions? Indeed, should certain criminals be allowed to earn successively better conditions through their behaviour? (10) It is a scientific fact that free will is unproven. In other words, the thoughts and actions of a person may well be due entirely to who they are genetically, the family in which they were raised, all their friends and acquaintances, and everything that has influenced them in any way: the media , events, chance encounters, etc. Hence, when we pass the death penalty we are actually not just punishing an individual for particular crime/s, but also for who they are genetically, physically, emotionally, and mentally; we are also punishing them for influences and chains of events over which they had no control. For this reason, the goal of a sentence cannot be revenge, but must be to protect society. If the purpose of execution is to exact revenge, it is fundamnetally wrong. (11) A death penalty is often not just a punishment of the person so sentenced, but also of their parents and siblings. Such a knock-on sentence is not desirable. (12) Due to all the checks and balances demanded by the finality of the death penalty, in the USA it is extremely expensive in terms of legal services and court time. Life sentences turn out to be much cheaper, and for the country as a whole, more economic than executions. (13) Having said all the above, many governments in the world have very little money, and cannot afford all the expensive medical equipment and drugs that are accessible in developed countries. Poor governments face stark choices between spending money on clean water, sewerage, education, medical care, anti-HIV drugs, Covid-19 vaccines, and electricity for innocent people, and spending money to keep sadistic multiple murderers alive in prison for decades. In such countries, it is in fact better to execute the worst kind of criminal quickly and cheaply, in order to save the lives of innocent people, even if a small number of innocent people is wrongfully executed each year.  Here endeth the lesson.

Mister Prager here doesn't understand how to discuss a topic by bringing up true arguments in favour of a thesis. What Prager does here is just showing a terrible crime, making the viewers emphatise with the victims, and then wishing to end the discussion with rethorical questions which don't really prove anything but that try to provoke an emotional reaction in the people who are watching. What the State is allowed to do is not something we should discuss trusting the instincts and the emotions, nor the common sense, but reason and logic. Why does the State punish certain people? The answer might look simple enough at first: because of justice, and justice means giving people what they deserve. But, unfortunately, that is an answer which refuges all explanations and actual possible uses in the real world. What does a person deserve? That's the issue: if we ask this question and we try to answer just using our instincts, there will be no way to find an agreement. Suppose that a man drives at 200 km/h on a road which lets you go at 70 km/h at most: the man is stopped by the police without causing harm to anyone else. What does the man deserve? One might say:" To be put into jail for a day or two, just because he might have killed someone if he got unlucky". Another person might argue that he doesn't deserve to be put into jail because he didn't actually harm anyone. What if the man was bringing his pregnant girlfriend to the hospital because she was about to give birth: would that matter? Would he deserve the same penalty? Some people might say yes, others might say that no, he wouldn't because he had a good reason to do what he did. And what if another man does exactly the same thign as the first one and he does harm someone while going at 200 km/h, does that make him more deserving of goign to jail or not? After all he did the same thing for the same reason, only that he got unlucky. It's easy to see that it's impossible to account for every factor and every opinion, and justice can't be based on opinions. The mere concept of "deserving something" implies that we all have free will and responsability of our actions, something that is not accepted by all cultures or philosophical currents. To me, the State shouldn't punish people because they deserve it, but to prevent the same crime in the future. Do those criminals deserve death penalty? I sincerely don't know, but why does it matter. Why does the State care about giving people what they deserve when it should care about preventing the same crime from happening? As stated by Cesare Beccaria in his book "About Crimes and Penalties", life sentence is much more effective in this regard, making the criminals a perpetuos example to the ones who want to repeat their actions. Death penalty doesn't, and also there is always the possibility that you might kill an innocent, who certainly doesn't deserve the punishment for the actions of another person. I hope that Prager readed this, so that he would come to understand why most people advocate for the abolishment of death penalty, but I honestly doubt that he would be willing to read something that would even for a moment go agaisnt his beliefs.

You cannot say something is justified unless you're basing the action off of an objective moral, something that doesn't change, something that is always true. The only objective morals are that of God. The morality of capital punishment follows the same principle as the morality of self-defense. If the execution of the murderer after he is apprehended is necessary to prevent more murderers, then capital punishment is justified, for the same reason it is right to disarm a murderer by deadly force before he is apprehended, while he is in the process of committing murder. But the important qualifier is if this is the only possible way. In most modern societies, defensive war is still necessary to repel foreign aggressors, for example, but capital punishment is not; life imprisonment in secure prisons without parole is sufficient to protect society.  If non-lethal means are sufficient to defend and protect people's safety from the aggressor, morally, authority must limit itself to such means, as these are more in keeping with concrete conditions of the common good and more in conformity with the dignity of the human person. Therefore, although capital punishment remains a public right if necessary, but it is not right under today's conditions. We have other ways of punishment, and we should be carrying out those ways to respect the dignity of the person.

I am opposed to the death penalty.... not the idea of it, but the reality of it.  Let me explain.  It's far, far more expensive than a lifetime of imprisonment.  If someone is condemned to execution, then they get all appeals, and that takes a long, long time and costs a lot of money.  The execution itself is extremely expensive.  It is less expensive to house an inmate to death, and that cost is transferred to the tax-payers.  Second, there is a chance that they are innocent, and any exculpatory evidence will necessarily be too late if they are dead.  Third, I don't trust the system.  With a success rate of I-don't-know-what... but assume upper 90 percent... that's still a very tragic thing to have happen: that a government should specifically murder one of its citizens wrongfully.  That sucks.  If the process was 100% guaranteed to be correct, it would still cost us more.  And to be perfectly blunt, I'm not willing to buy the difference between "we'll-never-see-him-again" and "we'll-never-see-him-again".

This just came up in my recommended feed, and it's quite old, but what the hell, here's my two cents worth:    Firstly, "Deserve to keep their lives" is an interesting phrasing, and reveals the troubling implication - that their, and by extension any other citizen's, lives are to be considered in the gift of the state, to be revocable at will.     There are basically 2 main thrusts in the anti death penalty argument:     1. That any convict, even after all appeals and with seemingly insurmountable evidence, could in fact be innocent, in which case EVERY CITIZEN, through its representative government, has unequivocally participated in a MURDER. Denying the death penalty is the only reasonable way to apply Blackstone's Ratio, reformulated by Benjamin Franklin as "it is better 100 guilty Persons should escape than that one innocent Person should suffer". Also, if someone is found innocent after 30 years in jail, they can be released, compensated and have some chance at a dignified life, a corpse cannot.    2. That to allow a death penalty is to give the government the right to kill its citizenry. As regards sanctification/cheapening, if the right to life is not absolute, then murder is not a crime based on a violation of a core principle, but rather usurpation of a right ( to kill people) that the government reserves for itself. "You have killed someone - no one has that right" is much more persuasive than "You have killed someone - do you have a license for that?"    I fully understand and accept that some people would want to kill murderers, rapists, etc. and especially when seeking revenge for loss of loved ones, but there is a difference between catharsis and justice, and there should be limits to the power that the citizenry grants to its government if it wants to be free. The best argument in favour of a death penalty is to conclude that one who violates another's right to life has waived their own, which provides a decent moral framework for personal revenge, but doesn't account for the 2 problems raised above. If you really cannot understand the opposition, as you say, then simply ask yourself this - is there anything you have seen in you life that you would be willing to state you were ABSOLUTELY certain of in every detail, knowing that if you were wrong members of your family would be killed? I rather doubt it.    Finally, practical considerations about actual murderers escaping or killing guards and prisoners don't factor into the moral argument, since both elements speak to fundamental truths of our society and aren't open to relativitic interpretation - whether 0 or 100 murderers escape to kill again has no bearing on whether the state should risk killing an innocent, nor about whether the state should have the right to kill its citizens. If murderers are escaping then prisons need reforming.

This whole debate suffers from a curse of poorly defined questions  Is the primary purpose of capital punishment:  1. To provide a deterrence to other people who may consider committing the crime in the future?  2. To provide a sense of closure, justice or revenge on behalf of the victim, their loved ones and the morally outraged in society?  3. To remove the threat of reoffending and thereby protect future potential victims   4. To make a moral statement about our collective identity about the type of society we want to live in.  If it’s 1.  Then the scale will slide, if it proves an effective deterrent then why not utilize it to deter other crimes.   If it’s 2 Then why are we against vigilante acts of retribution, why not expand justifiable homicide to anyone with a REALLY good reason  If it’s 3 Prison solves this problem, to some degree   If it’s 4  Then we are forced to confront the relativism by which we judge human life forfeit, and we need to take a hard look at the criteria by which we craft this ideal society.  I’d love to hear others thoughts on these points

If I could respond to Denis Prager directly. I'd remind him that by the beliefs of many Christians, no one deserves to live due to the hurt and sin they've caused. While it doesn't say that only God has the right to take a life, it does say that only God can pass judgement. Hence the argument for not taking their lives from a religious perspective.  That said... I think capital punishment could be applicable in some situations, not as a punishment, but as a consequence made in the interest of safety. It shouldn't be about 'justice' or 'revenge' because those things are subjective, but if a man shows no interest in seeking redemption after numerous terrible acts... surely it makes more sense to take their life so that they can't abuse it in order to keep taking lives from others. This is practical and rational. Not motivated by anger or revenge, but compassion, mercy and logic. Our actions must have consequences, and if Capital Punishment is not on the table, then it deprives the 'justice' system of a strong motivator towards actually making yourself useful or trying to seek redemption.  TLDR: I disagree with Denis' reasoning, but I believe that a case could be made that if you're clearly and willfully not going to do anything with your life besides be a destructive factor within society. (Not just a burden, but an active and terrible destroyer) then in that instance, I think a case could be made for capital punishment.

To be morally consistent pro life must mean pro life.  It’s the principle of capital punishment that I am personally opposed to in the same way I am opposed to abortion. Who are we to decide the value of life?  We are simply ill equipped, as humans, to make such judgments.  In other countries if a woman has an affair that is punishable by death (stoning). But the man than carries out that punishment - usually a family member, is not deemed a murderer.  Is this moral?  In other countries if a Muslim converts to Christianity ( akin to treason in a theocracy) that is punishable by death. Is this moral? Seeing as treason is punishable by death in the US also?  Or drug mules who carry the drugs that could lead to the overdose and death of hundreds? Is it moral to kill them also because of what they’re enabling?  Should the men in power who send their soldiers overseas to die be held accountable for those deaths and given the death penalty?  If North Koreans defectors are caught crossing the border they are shot on sight. That’s capital punishment, is it moral?  If capital punishment was carried out by being tied to a stake and set alight, is that moral?  What if it was lynching? Or cat-o-nine tails?  Is the executioner also a murderer?  Or what if the person convicted was literally mentally disabled? Which is all too often the case. Or had an careless legal team which was provided to them because they were poor?  Which, again, is all too often the case, and is not given a fair trial.   It’s the same question I ask the pro choicers: Who are you to decide the value of life?
